7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

So far in this series we’ve explored what HTTP QUERY is, how to implement it on the server, and how clients can use it. Now we get to the feature that makes QUERY genuinely valuable: caching.
This is also the easiest part of the series to understand. You don’t need to know much about HTTP internals to appreciate why it matters.
If you remember only one thing from this article, let it be this:
HTTP QUERY makes complex searches cacheable.
That means less work for your servers, fewer database queries, and applications that feel noticeably faster.
Let’s see why.
A Quick Refresher: What Is a Cache?
A cache is simply a temporary storage area for answers that have already been computed.
Instead of performing the same work every time someone asks the same question, the answer is stored somewhere nearby and reused.
Caches exist almost everywhere:

• Your browser

• CDNs like Cloudflare

• Reverse proxies

• API gateways

• Your own application
If a cache already has the answer, the request never reaches your server. The response is returned almost instantly.
The challenge has always been trust.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A cache serves previously computed responses whenever it can. Only cache misses reach the server.A cache must know that reusing a previous response is safe. It needs confidence that the request won’t change anything and that sending the exact same request again would produce the same result.
HTTP QUERY provides exactly those guarantees.
Why Search Was Traditionally Hard to Cache
For years, APIs had two choices.
Use GET
GET works perfectly with caches because the entire request is represented by the URL.
GET /products?category=books&author=tolkienFor simple searches, that’s ideal. The problem is that real-world searches rarely stay simple. Modern applications often need:

• dozens of filters

• nested conditions

• sorting options

• pagination

• complex search expressions
Trying to fit all of that into a URL quickly becomes impractical.
Use POST
The natural alternative was POST.
POST /products/search{
...
}
Now the search could be as large and structured as needed. Unfortunately, this solved one problem while creating another.
Caches generally assume a POST request might change server state.
Maybe it’s creating an order. Maybe it’s updating a user profile. Maybe it’s processing a payment.
Because caches can’t safely assume otherwise, they usually don’t reuse POST responses.
The result is that every search travels all the way to the server.
Even if ten thousand users perform exactly the same search, the application repeats the same work ten thousand times.
That’s a lot of unnecessary computation for requests that never modify any data.
How HTTP QUERY Changes the Picture
HTTP QUERY combines the advantages of both GET and POST.
Like POST, it supports a request body. Like GET, it is officially defined as safe and idempotent. Those two properties tell caches that the request is only reading data.
Once a response has been generated, it can safely be reused for identical requests.
The first request performs the work. Subsequent requests can often be answered directly from the cache.
For applications with many repeated searches, the performance improvement can be substantial.
Think about:

• dashboards

• product catalogs

• reporting systems

• analytics tools

• internal business applications
These systems often receive the same queries repeatedly throughout the day.
The One New Idea: The Request Body Is Part of the Cache Key
Press enter or click to view image in full size

With QUERY, the cache key is built from both the URL and the request body.This is the biggest conceptual difference between QUERY and GET.
With GET, the URL uniquely identifies the request.
GET /products?category=booksThe cache simply uses the URL as its lookup key.
QUERY is different.
QUERY /products/search{
"category": "books"
}
Multiple requests can use the same URL while asking completely different questions. That means the cache has to identify requests using both the URL and the request body.
Think of it like filing documents.
With GET, the folder name alone tells you which document to retrieve.
With QUERY, several different documents can live in the same folder, so you also need to read the document title before finding the correct one.
The cache works the same way. Before it can decide whether it already has an answer, it must examine the request body.
That requires a little more work than GET caching, but it’s still far cheaper than executing the search again.
Normalization: Improving Cache Hits
Two clients can send logically identical searches that look slightly different.
For example, one client might list JSON properties in a different order. Another might include extra whitespace.
The searches mean exactly the same thing, but their request bodies are not identical.
A simple cache would treat them as completely different requests. To avoid this, some caches normalize request bodies before generating the cache key.
Normalization converts equivalent requests into a consistent representation, allowing more searches to reuse the same cached response.
However, normalization should be conservative. If two genuinely different searches are accidentally treated as identical, the cache will confidently return incorrect results.
A few extra cache misses are much better than returning the wrong data.
A Better Optimisation: Give the Search Its Own URL
Earlier in this series, we discussed an elegant optimisation. Instead of repeatedly sending the full QUERY request, the server can assign that search its own permanent URL.
The client performs the QUERY once. The server responds with a URL representing that specific search.
From then on, the client simply performs a normal GET request against that URL.
The expensive search description is transmitted only once. Every future request benefits from the mature caching behavior that already exists for GET.
It’s similar to bookmarking a frequently used search.
Describe it once. Reuse it forever.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Freshness: How Long Should Cached Results Live?
Cached responses don’t last forever. Eventually the underlying data changes, so the server tells caches how long a response should be considered fresh.
You can think of this as a “best before” date.
For example:

• A product catalog that changes once a day can often be cached for an hour.

• Inventory information might only stay accurate for a few seconds.

• A live news feed may only be fresh for a minute or two.

• Real-time sports scores may not be cacheable at all.
There isn’t a single correct value. The right cache lifetime depends on two things:

• How frequently the data changes.

• How much stale data your users can tolerate.
Longer cache lifetimes reduce server load. Shorter lifetimes provide fresher data. Finding the right balance depends on your application.
Public vs Private Caches
Not every response should be shared. Some search results are identical for every user.
A public product search, for example, can safely be stored in a CDN. Other searches depend on the authenticated user.
Those responses belong only in that user’s browser cache.
Accidentally allowing personalized responses into a shared cache isn’t just a performance mistake.
It’s a privacy and security problem.
Revalidation: Asking Before Downloading Again
Even after a cached response expires, it may still be perfectly valid.
Instead of downloading everything again, the client can ask a much cheaper question:
“Has anything changed since I last requested this?”
If nothing has changed, the server responds with a lightweight 304 Not Modified response.
The client continues using its existing copy.
Only when the data has actually changed does the server send the full response again.
For dashboards that refresh every few seconds, this dramatically reduces bandwidth and unnecessary work.
Where Can QUERY Responses Be Cached?
A QUERY response can be cached at multiple layers.

• The browser can cache responses for a single user.

• A CDN can cache public responses for thousands of users.

• A reverse proxy can protect your application from repeated requests.

• Your application itself can cache expensive database queries.
As long as responses are labeled correctly with freshness information and the appropriate public or private visibility, every layer can contribute to improving performance.
A Practical Caching Strategy
You don’t need an elaborate caching system to benefit from HTTP QUERY.
Start with a few simple guidelines.

• Cache popular searches that don’t change frequently.

• Convert frequently repeated searches into permanent GET URLs whenever possible.

• Keep user-specific responses private.

• Use revalidation for pages that refresh often.

• Normalize request bodies carefully, and only when it’s safe.
These practices deliver most of the performance benefits without adding much complexity.
Common Mistakes
The most common mistake is allowing personalized search results into a shared cache. When in doubt, keep them private.
Another common problem is choosing cache lifetimes that don’t reflect how quickly the underlying data changes. Cache responses for too long and users see stale information. Cache them for too short a time and you lose much of the benefit.
Normalization can also cause trouble if it’s too aggressive. Treating different searches as identical leads to incorrect results.
Finally, remember that the request body is part of the cache key. If a cache starts returning results for the wrong search, an incomplete cache key is often the first thing to investigate.
What’s Next?
In Part 5, we’ll look at how to migrate existing POST-based search endpoints to HTTP QUERY without breaking existing clients.
You’ll see a practical migration strategy that lets older clients continue using POST while newer clients take advantage of QUERY’s safety, idempotency, and caching benefits.
